what_manipulated: linguistic framing of an activity as identity affirmation versus behavior
what_measured: intention or behavior to register and vote
experiment: Randomized experiments where survey phrasing was varied to frame voting either as personal identity enactment or as simple behavior. Units were survey respondents and voters. Experiments 1 used self-reported voter registration intention; experiments 2 and 3 examined actual voting behavior via state records. Statistical significance of differences between framing conditions was assessed.

what_manipulated: activation of religious versus neutral concept primes
what_measured: allocation of resources to anonymous others
experiment: Two experiments using an anonymous dictator game where units were subjects allocating money to strangers. Priming conditions included religious concepts, secular moral concepts, or control. Money allocation was measured as the outcome. Trait religiosity was also examined as a correlate.

what_manipulated: reminders or primes of religious concepts
what_measured: willingness to take nonmoral risks
experiment: Seven studies exposed units to reminders of a divine entity and measured risk-taking in nonmoral domains. Mediation analysis examined whether perceived danger of risky options mediated the effect. Study 8 examined affective responses after risky decisions with negative outcomes. Individual differences in perceived divine protection were analyzed as moderators.

what_manipulated: retrieval practice versus study repetition in learning phases
what_measured: recall performance on target items
experiment: Three experiments compared units in retrieval practice conditions versus repeated study conditions. Memory was tested via recall of target items. Experiment 1 found a negative testing effect; experiments 2 and 3 manipulated design features and obtained the typical positive effect. Results were interpreted via the multifactor account framework.

what_manipulated: modality of information (visual, auditory, or both) presented to raters
what_measured: judgment accuracy and preference regarding performance quality
experiment: Seven experiments had units judge music performance quality from different information modalities: silent video, sound only, or both. Outcomes included correct identification of actual competition winners and quality ratings. Comparisons examined whether judgments matched actual objective performance outcomes.

what_manipulated: current affective state or mood of raters
what_measured: self-reported well-being or life satisfaction judgment
experiment: Units in different mood states were asked to evaluate their overall life satisfaction. The study examined whether current mood influenced these global evaluative judgments. Analysis compared ratings across mood conditions.

what_manipulated: arousal level induced by environmental context (fear-arousing bridge versus neutral bridge)
what_measured: sexual attraction and sexual imagery in projective measures
experiment: Male units were recruited on either a fear-arousing suspension bridge or a control bridge and completed questionnaires with projective content. Sexual imagery in stories and attempt to contact the female interviewer were measured. A third study manipulated anticipated shock independently. Statistical comparisons examined differences between conditions.

what_manipulated: none; handedness is a measured individual difference; information provided about status quo and alternative options
what_measured: preference to maintain status quo versus switch to alternative
experiment: Two experiments presented units with hypothetical decision scenarios. Experiment 1 had units choose to stay with status quo or switch, measured across handedness groups. Experiment 2 manipulated the framing of information about alternatives as positive, negative, or equivocal. Responses were compared across handedness groups (consistent versus inconsistent right-handers).

what_manipulated: initial small request compliance, and whether the same or different person made the follow-up request
what_measured: compliance with a larger subsequent request
experiment: Two experiments used units in different request conditions. Experiment 1 had the same person make both requests (small initial, large follow-up). Experiment 2 had different people make the two requests. Compliance with the larger request was compared between units who had versus had not complied with the initial small request.

what_manipulated: taste of consumed substance (sweet, bitter, or water control)
what_measured: moral judgment severity regarding transgressions
experiment: Units consumed a beverage with sweet, bitter, or neutral taste and then rated the morality of various hypothetical transgressions. Ratings were compared across taste conditions. Analyses examined whether this effect differed by political ideology (conservative versus liberal).

what_manipulated: none; visual presentation structure (congruent objects-background versus incongruent)
what_measured: time to breakthrough to conscious awareness under perceptual suppression
experiment: Units experienced binocular rivalry with continuous flash suppression. Visual stimuli consisted of complex scenes with either congruent or incongruent object-background relationships. The latency to conscious perception of stimuli was measured as the dependent variable. Comparison examined whether incongruent stimuli broke through suppression faster or slower than congruent ones.

what_manipulated: color context (red versus blue) presented during tasks
what_measured: performance on detail-oriented versus creative cognitive tasks
experiment: Six studies examined how color exposure influenced cognitive performance. Study 1 measured motivational orientation (approach versus avoidance) induced by color. Studies 2 and 3 compared performance on detail-oriented versus creative tasks under different colors. Studies 4 and 5 replicated effects in product design and persuasive message domains. Study 6 examined whether effects occurred outside conscious awareness. Mediation analysis tested motivational activation as a mechanism.

what_manipulated: exposure to food type images (organic, comfort foods, or control)
what_measured: moral judgment severity and prosocial helping behavior
experiment: Units viewed images of organic foods, comfort foods, or control foods and subsequently rated moral transgressions and indicated willingness to volunteer time. Outcomes were time volunteered and moral severity judgments. Comparisons examined differences across food exposure conditions.

what_manipulated: none; ovulatory cycle phase of menstruating units
what_measured: political orientation and voting behavior
experiment: Two large, diverse samples of units tracked ovulation phase in relation to political orientation, religiosity, and reported voting intention in a presidential election. Analyses examined whether the relationship between ovulation and voting differed by relationship status (single versus committed).

what_manipulated: perceived personal control (reduced via threat induction or manipulation)
what_measured: belief in external systems (deity, government) and illusory control perception
experiment: Five studies examined the relationship between perceived personal control and system defense. Studies 1 and 2 manipulated control via threat and measured beliefs in a controlling deity. Study 3 analyzed cross-national data associating personal control perceptions with governmental support across 67 nations. Study 4 challenged external systems and measured illusory control. Mediators and moderators were identified to explain effects.

what_manipulated: none; distance between pairs in a memorized rank-order hierarchy
what_measured: response time and accuracy to status-related queries
experiment: Two experiments had units learn a rank-order status hierarchy (A > B > C > D) from a story. Units then answered queries about pairs at varying distances in the hierarchy. Response times and accuracy were measured as dependent variables. Experiment 1 provided explicit inference information; Experiment 2 required transitive inference during learning.

what_manipulated: framing of cost as tax-related versus non-tax equivalent cost; magnitude of cost
what_measured: preference to avoid or accept payment
experiment: Five experiments compared units' willingness to incur tax-related costs versus equivalent non-tax costs across different domains (retail purchases, financial investments, job selection). Preference or choice behavior was measured. Analysis examined whether effects were moderated by political identification. One study tested whether emphasizing positive uses of tax payments reduced tax aversion.

what_manipulated: auditory stimuli (word forms) paired with visual shapes under suppression or masking
what_measured: breakthrough time to awareness or visibility threshold of visual shapes
experiment: Multiple conditions used binocular rivalry suppression and backward masking. Units heard auditory word forms paired with visual shapes that were either phonologically congruent or incongruent. Latency to conscious detection and visibility thresholds of shapes were measured. Training manipulated letter-sound pairings to examine phonological versus visual feature contributions.

what_manipulated: structural congruity in language and music (congruent, moderately incongruent, or highly incongruent)
what_measured: event-related potential (brain electrical activity) at the P600 latency
experiment: Units were musically educated adults presented with sequences that violated or conformed to phrase structure (language) or harmonic rules (music). Event-related potentials were recorded from the scalp. Comparisons examined the magnitude and latency of positivities across modalities and levels of incongruity. Analysis tested whether P600-like components were specific to language.

what_manipulated: none; social class (lower versus upper)
what_measured: prosocial behavior including generosity, charity, trust, and helpfulness
experiment: Four studies compared units from lower and upper social classes on prosocial measures. Study 1 measured generosity in giving tasks; Study 2 examined charitable donations; Study 3 assessed trust in economic games; Study 4 measured helping behavior. Mediator analysis examined whether egalitarian values and compassion explained class differences.
